content:
# James Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 263 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_002_page_001.jpg

1. Brothers of me, not with partiality hold the faith of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos of the glory.

2. For if there should come into the assembly of you a man with a gold ring in splendid clothing, and there should also come in a poor [one] in filthy clothing,

3. and you should look upon the [one] wearing the splendid clothing and say, "You sit here well," and to the poor [one] you say, "You stand there," or "Sit under the footstool of me,"

4. did you not make distinctions among yourselves and become judges of evil reasonings?

5. Listen, beloved brothers of me: did not God choose the poor of the world, rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom which he promised to the [ones] loving him?

6. But you dishonored the poor [one]. Do not the rich oppress you, and they themselves drag you into courts?

7. Do not they blaspheme the good name, the [one] having been called upon you?

8. If indeed you fulfill [the] royal law according to the Scripture, "You shall love the neighbor of you as yourself," you do well;

9. but if you show partiality, you work sin, being convicted by the law as transgressors.

10. For whoever should keep the whole law but stumble in one [thing], he has become guilty of all.

11. For the [one] having said, "You shall not commit adultery," also said, "You shall not murder." And if you do not commit adultery but you murder, you have become a transgressor of [the] law.

12. So speak and so do as through [the] law of freedom being about to be judged.

13. For the judgment [is] merciless to the [one] not having done mercy; mercy boasts over judgment.

14. What [is] the profit, brothers of me, if someone should say he has faith, but should not have works? Is the faith able to save him?

15. If a brother or sister should be naked and lacking of the daily food,

16. and someone from among you should say to them, "Go in peace, be warmed and be filled," but you should not give to them the needful [things] of the body, what [is] the profit?

17. So also the faith, if it should not have works, is dead by itself.

18. But someone will say, "You have faith, and I have works." Show to me the faith of you apart from the works, and I will show to you from the works of me the faith.

19. You believe that God is one; you do well. Even the demons believe and shudder.

20. But do you wish to know, O empty man, that the faith apart from the works is idle?

21. Abraam the father of us, was he not justified from works, having offered up Isaak the son of him upon the altar?

22. You see that the faith was working together with the works of him, and from the works the faith was made complete,

23. and the Scripture was fulfilled, the [one] saying, "And Abraam believed God, and it was reckoned to him unto righteousness," and "friend of God" he was called.

24. You see that from works a man is justified and not from faith alone.

25. And likewise also Raab the harlot, was she not justified from works, having received the messengers and having sent [them] out by another way?

26. For just as the body apart from [the] spirit is dead, so also the faith apart from the works is dead.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

### Codex Sinaiticus Distinctive Readings (James 2)

**v. 1** — Sinaiticus reads the genitive chain as **"the faith of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos of the glory"** (τῆς πίστεως τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ τῆς δόξης). The phrase "of the glory" (τῆς δόξης) stands in apposition to "Yeshua Christos," identifying him as the Glory itself. Some scholars render this "our glorious Lord," but the literal construction places "the glory" as a separate genitive.

**v. 3** — Sinaiticus reads **"sit under the footstool of me"** (κάθου ὑπὸ τὸ ὑποπόδιόν μου). The Byzantine majority text reads "sit here under my footstool" with slightly different word order. The instruction to sit beneath the footstool is a mark of extreme social degradation.

**v. 4** — Sinaiticus reads **"did you not make distinctions"** (οὐ διεκρίθητε) as an aorist passive, "were you not divided" or "did you not discriminate." The verb διακρίνω in the passive can mean "to waver" or "to make distinctions."

**v. 5** — Sinaiticus reads **"the poor of the world"** (τοὺς πτωχοὺς τοῦ κόσμου) without the dative τῷ κόσμῳ ("poor to the world") found in some witnesses. The genitive "of the world" indicates origin or sphere: the poor belonging to this world.

**v. 14** — Sinaiticus reads **"Is the faith able to save him?"** (μὴ δύναται ἡ πίστις σῶσαι αὐτόν;). The article ἡ before πίστις is significant — James refers to "the faith" (that particular faith which lacks works), not faith in general.

**v. 18** — Sinaiticus reads **"apart from the works"** (χωρὶς τῶν ἔργων). Some later manuscripts read "from your works" (ἐκ τῶν ἔργων σου). The Sinaiticus reading creates a sharper challenge: show faith entirely divorced from works.

**v. 19** — Sinaiticus reads **"God is one"** (εἷς ἐστιν ὁ θεός), affirming the Shema (Deut 6:4). This is the standard reading across manuscript traditions.

**v. 20** — Sinaiticus reads **"idle"** (ἀργή) rather than **"dead"** (νεκρά) found in the Byzantine majority text and many later manuscripts. The word ἀργή means "idle, useless, unproductive" — literally "without work" (ἀ-εργός), creating a wordplay with ἔργα (works). This is a significant and likely original variant, as it is the more difficult reading and plays on the very word under discussion.

**v. 21** — Sinaiticus spells the patriarch's name as **Ἀβραάμ** (Abraam), the Hellenized form of the Hebrew אַבְרָהָם. Likewise **Ἰσαάκ** (Isaak) in the same verse.

**v. 25** — Sinaiticus reads **Ῥαάβ** (Raab) for the name Rahab. The designation **"the harlot"** (ἡ πόρνη) follows the LXX tradition (Joshua 2:1; 6:17). Sinaiticus reads **"messengers"** (ἀγγέλους), which could also be rendered "angels," but in this context refers to the human spies sent by Joshua.

### Scribal Features

The text is written in *scriptio continua* (continuous script without word divisions) in the characteristic four-column format of Codex Sinaiticus. The scribe (identified as Scribe A for this section) writes in a clear, regular uncial hand. No significant corrections by later hands are visible in this chapter. Line-end word breaks follow syllabic division. Nomina sacra are employed throughout: ΙΥ (Ἰησοῦ), ΧΥ (Χριστοῦ), ΚΥ (κυρίου), ΘΣ (θεός), ΠΝΣ (πνεύματος).
